svg.text: Feedback Loops: How the System Learns and Tightens
A systems view of runtime security improvement through guardrails, judges, humans, and downstream signals
Immutable log of every interaction, verdict, tool call, and outcome
FAST LOOP: Judge signals tighten guardrails
When the judge catches something guardrails missed, that pattern becomes a candidate
for a new deterministic rule. Converts expensive semantic checks into cheap pattern
matches. Each cycle makes guardrails smarter without increasing latency.
Delay impact: Low. Guardrails improve over hours, not seconds. Judge covers the gap in the meantime.
MEDIUM LOOP: Human labels calibrate the judge
Every human decision on an escalated case is a labeled data point. These labels
become ground truth for judge accuracy metrics. When the judge and humans
disagree, the judge prompt, thresholds, or model are adjusted.
Delay impact: Moderate. Until humans label enough cases, judge drift goes uncorrected.
MEDIUM LOOP: Human insight updates policy
Humans see what automation cannot: business context, user intent shifts, regulatory
changes. When reviewers notice a class of issue that neither guardrails nor judge
handle well, they escalate to policy owners for guardrail or judge scope changes.
Delay impact: Acceptable. Policy changes need deliberation. Speed here causes over-blocking.
SLOW LOOP: Downstream outcomes close the circle
Did the approved action succeed or cause harm? Downstream systems report outcomes:
transaction reversals, user complaints, compliance flags, error rates. These signals
flow back to recalibrate risk tiers, judge sensitivity, and escalation thresholds.
Delay impact: High. Harm may occur before the signal arrives. Mitigation: tighten controls for new use cases.
REINFORCING LOOP: Attack evolution vs. defense adaptation
(this loop never closes; it is managed, not solved)
No single layer is self-correcting. The guardrail does not know what it misses. The judge does not know when it drifts.
The human does not scale. Downstream outcomes arrive late. Each layer's weakness is another layer's feedback signal.
The feedback loops are the system. Without them, every layer degrades independently.
